name: Recursion
comment:
This module provides an abstraction over general recursion that allows the recursive computation
to be executed without risk of blowing the stack.

If you are unfamiliar with why we need to be careful about unsafe recursion in Elm,
[this article](https://functional-programming-in-elm.netlify.app/recursion/tail-call-elimination.html) describes the Tail-Call Elimination very well.

It is not terribly difficult to rewrite simple recursions to be safe by taking advantage of the Tail Call Optimization.
However, the moment you need to recurse on two or more elements in a data structure it becomes quite hairy to write safely
and the resulting code loses much of the beauty of recursion.

This module presents ways to create and execute the `Rec` monad, which is sufficiently powerful to represent non-simple recursion safely **and** will allow
you to preserve the recursive elegance that makes functional programming beautiful.


## Core Type

@docs Rec


## Creating a `Rec`

@docs base, recurse, recurseThen


## Manipulating a `Rec`

@docs map, andThen

Check out [`Recursion.Traverse`](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/micahhahn/elm-safe-recursion/2.0.0/Recursion-Traverse)
and [`Recursion.Fold`](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/micahhahn/elm-safe-recursion/2.0.0/Recursion-Fold)
for helpers that work with containers of recursive types.


## Running a `Rec`

@docs runRecursion


# Example

Imagine we have a generic binary tree type that we want to write a map function for:

    type Tree a
        = Leaf a
        | Node (Tree a) (Tree a)

The standard recursive map algorithm is straightforward:

    mapTree : (a -> b) -> Tree a -> Tree b
    mapTree f tree =
        case tree of
            Leaf a ->
                Leaf (f a)

            Node l r ->
                Node (mapTree f l) (mapTree f r)

⚠️⚠️⚠️ This is unsafe! ⚠️⚠️⚠️

Since the recursive calls to `mapTree` are not located in tail call position the Tail Call Optimization will not fire.
We are exposing ourselves to a crash if the tree is deep enough that we would have a stack overflow while executing it!


## Using elm-safe-recursion

    mapTree : (a -> b) -> Tree a -> Tree b
    mapTree f initTree =
        runRecursion
            (\tree ->
                case tree of
                    Leaf a ->
                        base (Leaf (f a))

                    Node l r ->
                        recurseThen l
                            (\newL ->
                                recurseThen r
                                    (\newR ->
                                        baes <| Node newL newR
                                    )
                            )
            )
            initTree

unions.name: Rec
unions.comment:
An opaque type representing a recursive computation.

  - `r` is the **r**ecursive type.
  - `t` is the **t**arget type that we are converting to.
  - `a` is a type for intermediate parts of the computation.

I think it is helpful to think of `Rec` like the `Promise` type in javascript. Simliar to a `Promise`, the
result in a `Rec` value might not be available yet because it needs to recursively evaluated in a separate step.
So instead of directly manipulating the value in a `Rec`, we instead can specify actions to be done with the value
when it is available using `map` and `andThen`.

values.name: andThen
values.comment: Apply a function to the result of a `Rec` computation that can specify more recursion.
values.type: (a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t a -> Recursion.Rec r t b

values.name: base
values.comment: The base case of a recursion. The value is injected directly into the `Rec` type.
values.type: a -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: map
values.comment: Apply a function to the result of a `Rec` computation.
values.type: (a -> b) -> Recursion.Rec r t a -> Recursion.Rec r t b

values.name: recurse
values.comment:
Recurse on a value.

When the recursion is complete the `Rec` will contain a value of type `t`.
values.type: r -> Recursion.Rec r t t

values.name: recurseThen
values.comment:
Recurse on a value and then take another action on the result.

If you find yourself writing code that looks like `recurse x |> andThen ...` or `recurse x |> map ...` you should
consider using `recurseThen` instead as it will be much more efficient.
values.type: r -> (t -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: runRecursion
values.comment: Run a recursion given a function to run one layer and an initial value.
values.type: (r -> Recursion.Rec r t t) -> r -> t

name: Recursion.Fold

values.name: foldArray
values.comment: Fold an `Array` whose items are recursive types.
values.type: (t -> a -> a) -> a -> Array.Array r -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: foldArrayThen
values.comment: Fold an `Array` whose items are recursive types and then perform a recursive action with the result.
values.type: (t -> a -> a) -> a -> Array.Array r -> (a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t b

values.name: foldDict
values.comment:
Fold a `Dict` whose values are recursive types.

    type HashTrie a
        = Leaf a
        | Node (Dict Char (HashTrie a))

    countHashTrie : HashTrie a -> Int
    countHashTrie =
        runRecursion <|
            \tree ->
                case tree of
                    Leaf _ ->
                        base 1

                    Node trees ->
                        foldDict (\_ x count -> x + count) 0 trees
values.type: (comparable -> t -> a -> a) -> a -> Dict.Dict comparable r -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: foldDictThen
values.comment: Fold a `Dict` whose values are recursive types and then perform a recursive action with the result.
values.type: (comparable -> t -> a -> a) -> a -> Dict.Dict comparable r -> (a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t b

values.name: foldList
values.comment:
Fold a list of items which are recursive types.

    type RoseTree a
        = Leaf a
        | Node (List (RoseTree a))

    countRoseTreeLeaves : RoseTree a -> Int
    countRoseTreeLeaves =
        runRecursion <|
            \tree ->
                case tree of
                    Leaf _ ->
                        base 1

                    Node trees ->
                        foldList (+) 0 trees
values.type: (t -> a -> a) -> a -> List.List r -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: foldListThen
values.comment: Fold a list of items which are recurisve types and then perform a recursive action with the result.
values.type: (t -> a -> a) -> a -> List.List r -> (a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t b

values.name: foldMapArray
values.comment: Fold an `Array` whose items contain recursive types.
values.type: (x -> a -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> a -> Array.Array x -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: foldMapArrayThen
values.comment: Fold an `Array` whose items contain recursive types and then perform a recursive action with the result.
values.type: (x -> a -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> a -> Array.Array x -> (a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t b

values.name: foldMapDict
values.comment:
Fold a `Dict` whose values contain recursive types.

    type HashTrie a
        = Leaf a
        | Node (Dict Char ( Int, HashTrie a ))

    countHashTrie : HashTrie a -> Int
    countHashTrie =
        runRecursion <|
            \tree ->
                case tree of
                    Leaf _ ->
                        base 1

                    Node trees ->
                        foldMapDict (\_ ( _, v ) count -> recurseMap v (\x -> x + count)) 0 trees
values.type: (comparable -> v -> a -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> a -> Dict.Dict comparable v -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: foldMapDictThen
values.comment: Fold a `Dict` whose values contain recursive types and then perform a recursive action with the result.
values.type: (comparable -> v -> a -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> a -> Dict.Dict comparable v -> (a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t b

values.name: foldMapList
values.comment:
Fold a list of items which contain recursive types.

    type KeyedRoseTree a
        = Leaf a
        | Node (List ( String, KeyedRoseTree a ))

    countRoseTreeLeaves : KeyedRoseTree a -> Int
    countRoseTreeLeaves =
        runRecursion <|
            \tree ->
                case tree of
                    Leaf _ ->
                        base 1

                    Node trees ->
                        foldMapList
                            (\( _, rec ) count -> recurseMap rec ((+) count))
                            0
                            trees
values.type: (x -> a -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> a -> List.List x -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: foldMapListThen
values.comment: Fold a list of items which contain recursive types and then perform a recursive action with the result.
values.type: (x -> a -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> a -> List.List x -> (a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t b

name: Recursion.Traverse

values.name: sequenceArray
values.comment: Traverse an `Array` where the values are recursive types.
values.type: Array.Array r -> Recursion.Rec r t (Array.Array t)

values.name: sequenceArrayThen
values.comment: Traverse an `Array` where the values are recursive types and then perform a recursive action on the result.
values.type: Array.Array r -> (Array.Array t -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: sequenceDict
values.comment:
Traverse a `Dict` where the values are recursive types.

    type HashTrie a
        = Leaf a
        | Node (Dict Char (HashTrie a))

    mapHashTrie : (a -> b) -> HashTrie a -> HashTrie b
    mapHashTrie f =
        runRecursion <|
            \tree ->
                case tree of
                    Leaf a ->
                        base (Leaf (f a))

                    Node dict ->
                        sequenceDict dict
                            |> map Node
values.type: Dict.Dict comparable r -> Recursion.Rec r t (Dict.Dict comparable t)

values.name: sequenceDictThen
values.comment: Traverse a `Dict` where the values are recursive types and then perform a recursive action on the result.
values.type: Dict.Dict comparable r -> (Dict.Dict comparable t -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: sequenceList
values.comment:
Traverse a list where the elements are recursive types.

    type RoseTree a
        = Node a (List (RoseTree a))

    mapRoseTree : (a -> b) -> RoseTree a -> RoseTree b
    mapRoseTree f =
        runRecursion <|
            \(Node a nodes) ->
                sequenceList nodes
                    |> map (Node (f a))
values.type: List.List r -> Recursion.Rec r t (List.List t)

values.name: sequenceListThen
values.comment: Traverse a list where the elements are recursive types and then perform a recursive action on the result.
values.type: List.List r -> (List.List t -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: sequenceMaybe
values.comment:
Traverse a `Maybe` where the value might be a recursive type.

    type NonEmpty a
        = NonEmpty a (Maybe (NonEmpty a))

    mapNonEmpty : (a -> b) -> NonEmpty a -> NonEmpty b
    mapNonEmpty f =
        runRecursion <|
            \(NonEmpty v maybe) ->
                sequenceMaybe maybe
                    |> map (NonEmpty (f v))
values.type: Maybe.Maybe r -> Recursion.Rec r t (Maybe.Maybe t)

values.name: sequenceMaybeThen
values.comment: Traverse a `Maybe` where the value might be a recursive type and then perform a recursive action on the result.
values.type: Maybe.Maybe r -> (Maybe.Maybe t -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: sequenceResult
values.comment: Traverse a `Result` where the success value might be a recursive type.
values.type: Result.Result e r -> Recursion.Rec r t (Result.Result e t)

values.name: sequenceResultThen
values.comment: Traverse a `Result` where the success value might be a recursive type and then perform an action on the recursive result.
values.type: Result.Result e r -> (Result.Result e t -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Recursion.Rec r t a

values.name: traverseArray
values.comment: Traverse an `Array` where the values contain recursive types.
values.type: (x -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Array.Array x -> Recursion.Rec r t (Array.Array a)

values.name: traverseArrayThen
values.comment: Traverse an `Array` where the values contain recursive types and then perform a recursive action on the result.
values.type: (x -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Array.Array x -> (Array.Array a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t b

values.name: traverseDict
values.comment: Traverse a `Dict` where the values contain recursive types.
values.type: (comparable -> v -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Dict.Dict comparable v -> Recursion.Rec r t (Dict.Dict comparable a)

values.name: traverseDictThen
values.comment: Traverse a `Dict` where the values contain recursive types and then perform a recursive action the result.
values.type: (comparable -> v -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Dict.Dict comparable v -> (Dict.Dict comparable a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t b

values.name: traverseList
values.comment:
Traverse a list where the elements contain recursive types.

    type KeyedRoseTree a
        = Node a (List ( String, KeyedRoseTree a ))

    mapKeyedRoseTree : (a -> b) -> KeyedRoseTree a -> KeyedRoseTree b
    mapKeyedRoseTree f =
        runRecursion <|
            \(Node a nodes) ->
                traverseList (\( s, tree ) -> recurseMap tree (Tuple.pair s)) nodes
                    |> map (Node (f a))
values.type: (x -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> List.List x -> Recursion.Rec r t (List.List a)

values.name: traverseListThen
values.comment: Traverse a list where the elements contain recursive types and then perform a recursive action on the result.
values.type: (x -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> List.List x -> (List.List a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t b

values.name: traverseMaybe
values.comment:
Traverse a `Maybe` where the value might contain a recursive type.

    type SeparatedList sep val
        = SeparatedList val (Maybe ( sep, SeparatedList sep val ))

    mapSeparatedList : (a -> b) -> SeparatedList sep a -> SeparatedList sep b
    mapSeparatedList f =
        runRecursion <|
            \(SeparatedList a maybeOthers) ->
                maybeOthers
                    |> traverseMaybe (\( sep, sepList ) -> recurseMap sepList (Tuple.pair sep))
                    |> map (SeparatedList (f a))
values.type: (x -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Maybe.Maybe x -> Recursion.Rec r t (Maybe.Maybe a)

values.name: traverseMaybeThen
values.comment: Traverse a `Maybe` where the value might contain a recursive type and then perform a recursive action on the result.
values.type: (x -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Maybe.Maybe x -> (Maybe.Maybe a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t b

values.name: traverseResult
values.comment: Traverse a `Result` where the success value might contain a recursive type.
values.type: (v -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Result.Result e v -> Recursion.Rec r t (Result.Result e a)

values.name: traverseResultThen
values.comment: Traverse a `Result` where the success value might contain a recursive type and then perform an action on the recursive result.
values.type: (v -> Recursion.Rec r t a) -> Result.Result e v -> (Result.Result e a -> Recursion.Rec r t b) -> Recursion.Rec r t b
